data.title: Mastercard's Earnings Preview: It's A Buy Once Again
data.description:
I've been Mastercard's (MA) shareholder for years, and I keep increasing my position from time to time. In my eyes, it's one of the best business models ever created. I know it's not the leadership player, as it's still behind Visa (V) (which I also own), but I see MA as a strong challenger. What do I like about MA as compared to V? While it has a smaller scale, it grows faster.

Still, I put MA on hold the last time I covered it (in October last year). Why? I felt like the valuation was too high given the volatile market environment and quite high expectations for future results.

I'm happy I stopped buying, as I saw MA's stock price decline over the last 6 months. It dropped by c.a. 12% since the last time I talked about MA. At the same time, the broad index grew by nearly 4%, so MA was quite an underperformer, wasn't it?

Now, I'd like to provide you with a follow-up given the upcoming earnings release. I'll share my thoughts on the upcoming results and the shift in valuation.

But I will say this right from the get-go: I'm upgrading MA to a Buy. Yes, I recognize the valuation is higher than V's. But I think the risk-to-reward profile is much more attractive now.

MA's Earnings Preview: Positive Outlook Despite Slower Growth Expectations

Before I discuss the upcoming earnings release, I will take a step back and take a quick look at the most recent data. I was happy to see a relatively high double-digit revenue growth (vs. MA's previous quarters). At the same time, operating expenses grew more slowly. What's the result? What I like to see the most, higher operating margin, which is one of the best profitability levels in the market. Yes, in the market. Not just in the sector.

Ok, let's now discuss the upcoming earnings release. I think it will be positive for MA, but I don't think it will be the catalyst for stock price growth. The market is now much more careful than it was last year, but that's where I see opportunities like MA. What's the management's outlook for Q1 2026? I'm pasting it below from the last Earnings Call:

Now turning to the first quarter of 2026. Year-over-year net revenue growth is expected to be at the low end of a low double-digit range on a currency-neutral basis, excluding inorganic activity. We estimate a tailwind of approximately 3.5 to 4 ppt from foreign exchange for the quarter. From an operating expense standpoint, we expect Q1 growth to be in the high end of high single-digit range versus a year ago, again, on a currency-neutral basis, excluding inorganic activity and special items. Foreign exchange is forecasted to be a headwind of approximately 2.5 ppt for the quarter.

So the revenue growth will likely be slower than last quarter. How fast did MA grow in the first quarter last year? I see 14%, so I don't think MA will be able to match that this time.

But I could be too pessimistic. The market expects a 13.87% growth rate (27 analysts came up with an average estimate of quarterly revenue of $8.26B). Still, I don't think it will happen; I think the growth will be lower (from 12% to 13%).

How about profitability? As the management indicated that they expect high single-digit growth of expenses, I expect improved profitability. Even with lower revenue growth than the market expects. So in total, I think the quarterly update will be positive.

The question is, will that spark a stock price recovery? I don't think so. After such a dynamic drop in valuation (I discuss it below), I believe the market needs more time to appreciate MA.

The Valuation Makes The Risk-To-Reward Profile Not Attractive

I mentioned earlier that the valuation is more attractive. It's still higher than V's, but I think there's a good reason behind it. I will discuss that from the perspective of multiple valuations. But it's not as simple as comparing two numbers and saying, "V is a better buy, because it has a lower multiple". I always remind them that context is key.

Look at the chart below. Both MA and V experienced a sharp valuation drop. The forward-looking P/E ratios dropped from the range of 30-35x in the first half of 2025. Now? They trade at around 25x. MA at 25.7x and V at 24x, so yes, MA seems more expensive than V. But I'd like to remind you that MA grows faster than V. Even last quarter, MA grew around 300 bps faster than V (18% vs 15%).

So, focusing on MA itself, I think it's quite an attractive buy right now. The multiple is around 26% lower now than it was just 6 months ago. While I expect quite a "calm" earnings release with moderate growth, I believe MA's long-term risk-to-reward profile is attractive.

That's why I think MA is a buy. Valuation is more compelling, and the business is as good as ever. I expect double-digit total returns for those who are willing to hold it longer. To put my money where my mouth is, I've just bought more shares in MA.

data.title: Analyst Connect April 2026: A Guide For Conducting Investment Research
data.description:
Investment Research Best Practices: Our Resources for Analysts and Readers

We recently came across a resource provided several years ago to analysts and readers, an article presenting details and suggestions on how to approach investment analysis. And we’ve updated that article here.

The key takeaways: Research approaches evolve for the best analysts. There’s always room for improvement. And make sure to keep an open mind and be receptive to other ideas when it comes to sharpening research skills around investment ideas.

We asked analyst Thomas Lott to share his investment research process in this article from August, which may provide another perspective for analysts and readers.

We also have other resources available on our Analyst Connect article page.

The Positives of Presenting Previous Coverage in Follow-Up Articles

We encourage analysts to present follow-up coverage of stocks and investments they analyze for readers. As part of that follow-up effort, we ask analysts to mention previous coverage of a stock they covered in a past article.

There are benefits to referencing past articles in a new article covering a stock. The mention helps remind readers what was covered in the last article, presents a platform for outlining what may have changed (or not changed) since the previous analysis, and what may happen next for the investment in focus. Analysts also benefit from additional pageviews when readers are provided access to an older article.

The reference to past coverage should be at the start of the article. The link to that earlier article and the reference do not need to be in the first paragraph (though this is strongly encouraged) but should be included in the second or third paragraph.

Reference to past coverage is a key component of our follow-up article guidelines.

Our guidelines for follow-up coverage of an investment:

  • The follow-up focuses on a key material development or news that could impact the investment premise. This should go beyond just a change in sentiment or rating. And the follow-up should avoid any recap of any new development: The analysis should present a forward-looking angle on how a new development may impact the investment idea.

  • The follow-up article should advance the investment narrative. Avoid the use of old data or repurposed content from a previous article. Essentially, the new submission should present something fresh for reader consideration.

  • An analyst does not need to present a new rating or sentiment around a material change or catalyst that could impact the premise. But the analyst needs to clearly explain the reason for follow-up coverage, not only for readers but also for editors. Provide a note to editors explaining the need for follow-up coverage.

Our follow-up guidelines can be found here.

Looking for Your Best Commodity-Focused Investment Idea

Do you have high conviction behind a commodity-focused investment idea?

Seeking Alpha is inviting analysts to present their analysis of a commodity idea for its latest article competition. This includes stocks and ETFs. Inverse ETFs are not part of the competition, and the investment vehicle must trade in the US or Canada and have a ticker page on our site.

Analysts can send in an exclusive article with their idea. Add a comment in the "note to the editor" section requesting that the article is entered into the competition. The deadline is midnight, May 31.

Details on the competition can be found here.

Assessing the Energy Business: Tips and Details on Evaluating the Sector

The energy industry is big, with several moving parts within each sector. Valuating investments requires knowledge of the companies operating within the energy sphere. And how investors look at energy companies is influenced by the constantly changing geopolitical scene.

Seeking Alpha analyst Power Hedge, the Investing Group leader behind the Energy Profits in Dividends service, provides details on the different parts of the energy industry, how to evaluate companies within the sector, and how to assess the impact of macro developments on the business.

Power Hedge provides this primer and an assessment of the energy industry here.

Looking Ahead to May

May sees the earnings calendar get busier in the first few weeks, but it hits a peak in week two. The macro story surrounding the war in the Middle East continues to weigh on the market, and each day seems to add to the uncertainty. The economic reports that are released in May will reflect the impact the war is having on a number of different economic indicators, and this could create more volatility.

Seeking Alpha Earnings Calendar

Week 1 (May 1-8)

Even though May kicks off on a Friday, it comes with a number of big earnings reports before the open. At the top of the list, both in terms of market cap and potential importance, are Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX). These two energy behemoths should show some benefits from the elevated oil prices that started in March. In addition to these icons, we will get reports from Aon (AON), Colgate-Palmolive (CL), and Dominion Energy (D), among others. Shifting our focus to Monday, May 4, we expect to hear from AI industry darling Palantir (PLTR), Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), The Williams Companies (WMB), and ON Semiconductor (ON), among the many.

Looking out to Tuesday, and the docket just gets busier. Among the companies expected to report are Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Arista Networks (ANET), Shopify (SHOP), Eaton (ETN), and Pfizer (PFE). Looking at Wednesday and Thursday, we see the highest totals of companies for the entire earnings season, with 356 and 443 reports expected, respectively. For Wednesday, the biggest names are Disney (DIS), Arm Holdings (ARM), AppLovin (APP), Uber (UBER), and Marriott (MAR). For Thursday we expect to hear from household names such as Shell (SHEL), McDonald’s (MCD), Airbnb (ABNB), Monster Beverage (MNST), and Warner Bros. Discovery, among many others. Friday has a handful of big reports expected, and they're led by Toyota (TM), Sony (SONY), AngloGold Ashanti, and EchoStar (SATS).

Week 2 (May 11-15)

This is where the dates of the reports become a little less certain, as many companies don’t announce the exact date of earnings until two to three weeks ahead of the report. Monday shows Constellation Energy (CEG), Fox Corporation (FOXA), and Circle Internet Group (CRCL) as reports we should expect. Looking out to Tuesday, Sea Limited (SE), Vodafone (VOD), AST Spacemobile (ASTS), and Venture Global (VG) are among the most highly followed companies reporting. Wednesday is highlighted by Cisco Systems (CSCO), Sumitomo Mitsui (SMFG), Takeda Pharmaceuticals (TAK), and Honda Motor Co. (HMC). Thursday features a couple of tech heavyweights with Alibaba (BABA) and Applied Materials (AMAT) expected to report, along with JD.Com (JD) and Copart (CPRT). The only names that jumped out for Friday were Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group (MUFG) and RBC Barings (RBC).

Week 3 (May 18-22)

Monday, May 18, has a few reports investors will likely be interested in, starting with Trip.com (TCOM) and Ryanair (RYAAY). EV manufacturer XPeng (XPEV) is also on the docket. We see a shift in the focus on Tuesday as retail names start to step into the earnings confessional. The group is led by Home Depot (HD), Target (TGT), and Urban Outfitters (URBN). We also expect to hear from PDD Holdings (PDD) and Keysight Technologies, among others. Wednesday is arguably the biggest date in the second half of the month, with Nvidia (NVDA) on the docket along with Analog Devices (ADI), Palo Alto Networks (PANW), and Snowflake (SNOW) from the tech sector. In addition to the tech names, retailers are in the spotlight with The TJX Companies (TJX) and Lowe’s (LOW) expected to report.

The retail theme carries over to Thursday with the likes of Walmart (WMT), Ross Stores (ROST), Ralph Lauren (RL), and BJ’s Wholesale (BJ) joining the fray. We also see Deere & Company (DE) and Intuit (INTU) on the calendar. Friday only brings a few reports, with HEICO (HEI), Zoom Communications (ZM), and Williams Sonoma (WSM) being the most prominent.

Week 4 (May 25-29)

US markets will be closed on Monday, May 25, in observance of Memorial Day. For Tuesday we see a few reports scheduled with AutoZone (AZO), Autodesk (ADSK), Agilent (A), Workday (WDAY) and Dick’s Sporting Goods (DKS) among the biggest. Moving out to Wednesday, we get a few from the tech sector once again, with Salesforce (CRM), Synopsis (SNPS), and Everpure (P) leading the charge.

Thursday brings reports from some pretty big names in terms of market cap. We see Costco (COST), Dell Technologies (DELL), Marvell Technology (MRVL), and NetEase (NTES) among the most notable. Friday’s calendar shows Zscaler (ZS), MongoDB (MDB), and HP Inc. (HPQ) as possibly reporting, but that doesn’t seem likely, and those dates are estimates.

data.title: A Massive Change Likely Coming To The Fed And Markets May Not Like It
data.description:
Major changes may be coming to the Fed in a few short weeks, and more importantly, this week's Fed meeting could very well be the end of an era, as Jay Powell takes to the podium one last time.

It is unclear what exactly a Kevin Warsh-led Fed will look like, but now that the charges against Jay Powell have been dropped, we are likely to find out soon.

There is, of course, what Kevin Warsh would like to change versus what ultimately the market will allow him to change.

If Kevin Warsh has his way, the biggest change likely to come could be the end of the quarterly dot plot. Warch specifically noted in his Senate hearing that he is no fan of forward guidance. His belief is that part of the reason for the mistakes in 2021 and 2022 was that the Fed gave forward guidance, which ultimately compounded the problem because it held on to its views for too long. He believes that the Fed should wait until it reaches the actual meetings to make a decision, which he feels can keep the central bank from repeating its errors.

It may actually also result in fewer Fed speaking events in the future. He actually takes that even further later in his testimony, noting that the Fed should not be previewing what a future decision will be. He also notes that Fed officials should not be sharing views on where rates should be and thinks it is unhelpful.

Balance Sheet Is Too Big

Warsh also made clear he is no fan of the Fed using its balance sheet, calling it fiscal policy in disguise and noting that the Fed owns more outstanding debt than some parts of the financial markets. He also noted that those with financial assets have benefited from the balance sheet expansion, and he views interest rate policy as more even, getting into what he said are the "cracks of the economy."

He recalled his time as a Fed governor, when the balance sheet was first expanded in 2008, and that the transmission mechanism for a large balance sheet was through asset prices and signaling. According to him, only about half of Americans have financial assets, and the interest rate policy is a more even and fair way to help all Americans.

It most likely means that quantitative easing will be used during times of crisis, and that more likely than not, a smaller Fed balance sheet is coming, one that has a less dominant footprint on markets. However, the market will challenge him on this; we saw a preview in the fall of 2025.

A smaller Fed balance may also mean fewer reserve balances held at the Fed, which may mean that Warsh has limited scope here. We already saw in the fall of 2025 that reserve balances below the $3 trillion level were starting to negatively impact the funding market, with the secured overnight funding rate trading above the interest on reserve balances.

Additionally, the use of the standing repo facility increased during that time, though not notably, and it ultimately led the Fed to buy Treasury bills in December to boost reserves heading into Tax season.

Of the policy changes Warsh wants, shrinking the balance sheet will be the most challenging. Reserves have already proven to be close to the low end of ample, and while Warsh may have some scope to shrink the balance sheet some, he likely doesn't have much room. He could allow this to happen mostly by not expanding the balance sheet and maintaining the size at current levels while allowing changes in liabilities to naturally drain off reserves in a very slow, meticulous process. Given the current state of the balance sheet, there is a risk that something will break in the broader market.

New Inflation Measure

Warsh may also change the tools the Fed uses to measure inflation. Right now, under Jay Powell, the preferred inflation metric is the core PCE, which is still running at around 3% annualized. Warsh's preferred gauge sounds like it may be the trimmed-mean PCE. That is where the biggest outliers are removed from the reading. That would be the biggest change, perhaps, because the trimmed-mean PCE, as measured by the Dallas Fed, is currently just 2.33%.

Currently, the trimmed mean PCE is running below the core PCE rate, but between 2012 and 2021, the trimmed mean PCE was consistently at or above the core PCE rate.

How the Market Might Adjust

What this may mean, if this policy actually starts to be pushed out, is a much steeper yield curve, driven by higher term premiums on longer-end rates. It may also mean much more volatility in short-term rates, as the market moves and adjusts much more quickly to economic data, especially if the dot plot and forward guidance are removed. In fact, based on the trimmed mean PCE, one could argue for rate cuts as soon as he takes over. If that is the case, then perhaps short-term rates are too high.

More importantly, lower liquidity, higher implied volatility, and a steeper yield curve, driven by higher term premiums, could mean wider credit spreads and, perhaps more importantly, tighter financial conditions. Financial conditions have been remarkably loose in recent years and have no doubt played a key role in driving stock market multiples significantly higher.

The biggest takeaway is not that Warsh will make major changes, but that the market may ultimately determine how far he can go.

We know that the Fed is already operating close to the lower bound of ample reserves, as seen in funding market behavior in late 2025, when SOFR became more volatile and usage of the standing repo facility increased. It suggests there is limited room to further reduce the balance sheet without destabilizing funding markets.

In that sense, the constraint on Warsh's changes may not come from the Fed itself, but from the market.

data.title: 3 Best Gold Stocks With 110%+ Forward Growth For The Next Leg Up
data.description:
Gold Stocks Primed for a Post-Iran Macro Backdrop

Gold’s recent price movements have been influenced less by its traditional drivers and more by the shifting headlines around the Iran conflict. In the near term, escalation has lifted oil, pushing inflation expectations and real yields higher. This has strengthened the U.S. dollar and placed downside pressure on gold. Conversely, news of de-escalation has eased those pressures, allowing gold, especially the high-quality miner stocks, to rebound. This is an inversion of its typical safe haven role. Yet, despite the headwind from elevated real yields, gold has held up relatively well, indicating potential for a post-conflict rally for gold and miner stocks.

Negative Relationship Between Gold and US 'Real Yields'

Gold’s recent price movement and its resilience reflect a supportive macro backdrop after the Iran conflict settles:

  • Fed rate cuts
  • Weakening dollar
  • Sticky inflation
  • Central bank buying

As the conflict resolves and the oil-driven inflation pressures and volatility fade, markets are likely to refocus on these fundamentals, particularly the direction of real interest rates. This sets up a strong environment for gold's next leg higher, with the greatest beneficiaries being gold miners with sector-leading forward growth metrics.

How I Chose the Best Gold Stocks for a Post-Iran Rally

To select the best gold stocks to feature in this article, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener and chose the pre-selected Top Materials Stocks and filtered for Quant Strong Buys. I then sorted by Industry and Sector and arrived at three companies in the gold industry with at least 100% forward EBIT.

1. Coeur Mining, Inc. (CDE)

  • Market Capitalization: $19.52B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/24/2026): 33 out of 282

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/24/2026): 8 out of 49

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Gold

One of several Strong Buy materials stocks in my Alpha Picks portfolio, Coeur Mining is well positioned as a high-beta play in a strengthening macro backdrop supporting gold. The company’s North American focus helps minimize geopolitical risk relative to other miners. CDE’s high-margin gold mines and increasing exposure to silver also provide potential for further upside after the Iran conflict cools. From a Quant perspective, CDE stands out with strong Growth and Profitability Factor Grades and supporting metrics.

Coeur Mining’s 'A+' Growth Grade was a key determining factor when I decided to add it to my Alpha Picks portfolio. The company’s forward EBIT growth is expected at 179.43%, which is more than 15x the sector median. CDE’s 174.40% forward Operating Cash Flow Growth also highlights extreme fundamental strength supported by rising production and strong profit margins.

Supporting CDE’s forward growth is its sector-crushing margins, which support the 'A' Profitability Grade. A clear sign of fundamental strength for a gold miner is Return on Capital, and CDE’s measure of 17.09% is over 218% higher than the sector median. This indicates that the company is allocating capital efficiently, which differentiates CDE from competitors that only look good when gold prices are high. In a macro environment headed toward falling real rates and a weaker dollar, Coeur’s earnings growth and profitability become a key advantage. That leverage sets up a similarly positioned miner.

2. SSR Mining Inc. (SSRM)

  • Market Capitalization: $6.46B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/24/2026): 13 out of 282

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/24/2026): 3 out of 49

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Gold

One of my PRO Quant Portfolio miner holdings, SSR Mining offers a diversified asset base across North America and Turkey. SSRM’s forward growth recently gained a tailwind with the sale of its 80% ownership stake in the troubled, non-operating Copler gold mine in Turkey for $1.5B. As macro conditions shift toward lower real yields and central bank demand, SSR Mining’s growth and profitability can continue to expand.

Like CDE, SSRM boasts forward EBIT growth well above 100%, which represents more than 10x the sector median. Also supporting its fundamental strength outlook is its 223% forward Operating Cash Flow Growth, which is more than 19x the sector median. Further supporting more room to run for SSRM, its forward P/E of 7.29 represents more than a 54% discount to sector peers.

Enhancing SSRM’s fundamental strength is the company’s ‘A-’ Profitability Grade. Since mining is a capital-intensive industry, SSRM’s 7.41% Return on Total Capital demonstrates strength in allocating capital, while its sector-leading Levered FCF Margin of nearly 20% indicates that SSRM has plenty of cash left after sustaining the business and servicing debt. SSRM’s disciplined cost structure enables the company to convert higher gold prices into free cash flow, reinforcing its attractiveness in a rising gold environment. This balance between growth and stability leads to another miner with similar upside potential.

3. Centerra Gold Inc. (CGAU)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.72B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/24/2026): 8 out of 282

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/24/2026): 2 out of 49

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Gold

Centerra Gold, another one of my PRO Quant Portfolio holdings, exhibits similar strengths as our other two gold stocks, including strong liquidity and a self-funded growth pipeline, supporting further upside after the Iran conflict eases. As macro tailwinds, including easing monetary policy and a softer dollar, begin to take hold, Centerra’s operating leverage can drive more earnings growth.

CGAU’s growth metrics allow for a distinction between primary and secondary measures of fundamental strength. While the company’s -12-13% year-over-year Working Capital Growth does reflect some liquidity strain, this metric is more cyclical and does not automatically reflect poorly on long-term value creation. What’s more important from an investor perspective is its forward EBIT growth above 110% and forward Operating Cash Flow Growth of nearly 25%, which is more than double the sector median.

CGAU’s 'A-' Profitability Grade is supported by a range of metrics, starting with its impressive Net Income Margin of 42.18%, which is more than 8x the sector median. This tells us that Centerra is doing a superior job of converting revenue into bottom-line profit much more efficiently than its peers. Like CDE and SSRM, CGAU shows strength in sector-leading Return on Total Capital, which is nearly 20% on a trailing basis. Together, these characteristics help frame the broader opportunity for gold mining industry leaders.

Conclusion: Gold Stocks Set to Shine With Forward Growth

The recent divergence between gold’s traditional drivers and its price movement on Iran conflict headlines has created a compelling setup for gold miners. As geopolitical tensions resolve, investor attention is likely to shift back to the pre-conflict macro conditions, which include Fed rate cuts by late 2026, a weaker U.S. dollar, and sustained demand from central banks and investors. In that environment, gold stocks like CDE, SSRM, and CGAU offer leveraged exposure to gold through strong forward earnings and expanding profit margins. However, risks remain. A prolonged conflict with Iran could hold oil prices, inflation, and the dollar higher for longer, delaying rate cuts and placing further downside pressure on gold and miners. Even so, the longer-term outlook for growth-oriented gold stocks remains.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Subscribe to Premium for unlimited access to Seeking Alpha quant ratings and expert content. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" Quant stocks. Check out our new Alpha Picks app in the Apple App Store! Seeking Alpha's quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you're looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It's a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy investing!

data.title: Stable Income In Uncertain Times: The Hidden Opportunity In RIV And RIV.PR.A
data.description:
As markets become more unpredictable, many people are wondering how to preserve their money and even earn a regular income from it. One option that has been gaining interest recently is the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund (RIV), which invests in a variety of assets and distributes income to its investors as dividends. Simply put, you buy a share, they manage the portfolio, and you get a regular payment. In the following lines, we will give you more details about the fund, and if you are interested in our last article and what the things were, then you can read it here.

Below is shown a comparison of the fund to the overall market:

And here you can see where it stands among its peers:

For those looking for even more predictable income, the fund also offers a preferred stock, RIV.PR.A. More information can be found in the article a little below.

RIV was founded on 12/24/2015 and follows a tactical asset allocation strategy. The fund invests primarily in CEFs (42%), followed by SPAC (34%), Investment company debt (11%), and BDCs (6%). As of the end of 03/31/2026, the fund's net assets amounted to approximately $324 million, with 442 positions in the portfolio.

The largest part of the portfolio to asset class allocation is held by SPACs, 35%, followed by US equities, 20%, investment company bonds, and fixed income, 11% each. The fund also holds 7% in International equities and 6% in BDCs. In the next screenshot, you can see the top 10 of its investments:

Pershing Square Holdings leads with a part of 9.42%, followed by iShares Flexible Income ETF (3.84%) and BlackRock Science & Tech Trust II (3.13%).

The total leverage ratio of the fund is close to 24.10%, the expense ratio is around 3.76%, and the non-leverage expense ratio is close to 1.77%:

RIV is currently traded at a 3.7% discount. It has a positive 1-year Z-score of 0.42 and a positive 3-year Z-score of 0.49. The premium/discount history of the fund for the past 10 years is as follows:

RIV's NAV's 3-year average annualized total returns are close to 12%, and its 5-year returns are around 6.5%.

Preferred Stock

RIV.PR.A is a cumulative perpetual stock that was issued on 04/12/2022 with a coupon rate of 6% and a spread to the treasury yield then of close to 3.2%. The annual amount of its cumulative distributions is $1.5, paid $0.375 quarterly. At the time of writing, the stock is traded below par, and its current yield is close to 6.59%, and the spread to the treasury yield is around 1.66% - the narrowed spreads are quite normal for the overall market and market environment currently. The stock is rated with an "A1" credit rating by Moody's. It has close to 1 year to call, with a call date on 05/15/2027. CEFs are required to maintain limited leverage, and the stock is under the regulation of the 1940 Act:

In the next chart, you can observe the potential prices and potential yields to worst of the stock:

If we compare RIV.PR.A with its peers - fund preferred stocks, that trade below par with similar credit ratings—we see that the stock is in the highest yields:

Conclusion

RIV is an interesting vehicle for investors seeking diversification through a CEF structure. Its preferred stock, RIV.PR.A, stands out as a particularly attractive option for more conservative portfolios - high credit rating, regulatory protection, and current yield of approximately 6.6%.

data.title: FEPI: A Battle-Tested 25% Yielder To Buy
data.description:
Back in early January of this year, I issued a bullish article on the REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (FEPI). The case boiled down to increasing volatility, which, for FEPI, a covered call ETF vehicle, should translate to richer income distributions. In other words, the combination of sky-high valuations and an increasingly shaky geopolitical environment was, in my view, the perfect recipe for heightened volatility.

I guess it would be fair to say that this prediction has come to fruition.

Just take a look at VIX YTD development, which measures the implied volatility of the S&P 500 (SPY).

Yet the performance of FEPI has not been that great. The ETF is up by ~1% on a YTD and total return basis, while SPY and the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ), which has the strongest correlation to FEPI's underlying exposures, have advanced by 4% and 6%, respectively.

Moreover, the volatility has led to temporarily unfavorable repricing across all major equity indices (especially for QQQ); see the dividend per share dynamic since January 2026):

Having said that, it would be wrong to interpret this dynamic as a signal that FEPI is just another yield trap, which performs well as long as there are some tailwinds in place.

In my opinion, this recent performance of FEPI reveals a couple of interesting items, which have to be appreciated by investors who have or who plan to add FEPI to their income-seeking portfolios.

Let me explain.

Thesis Review

As we can see in the chart below, FEPI has lagged the Nasdaq-100 and, price-wise, is down by ~5.5%.

The monthly ~25% yielding dividend payments have compensated for the realized share price downside and provided a slight positive return result. Yet, as stated earlier in the intro section, these dividends have been decreasing as well, which does not send too encouraging a message, to say the least.

Now, we have to understand why this has happened.

Two things.

First, FEPI's portfolio exposure to high-beta names (strong growth, richly priced AI security) has not come in handy so far this year. Just take a look at what top holdings look like for FEPI:

Namely, there is a huge difference between the Nasdaq-100 and FEPI's portfolio. The difference is that FEPI holds a) more pronounced exposures to high-growth AI (and technology) and b) much more concentrated positions than those that we can find in the QQQ's mix. For instance, while QQQ is comprised of 100 individual stocks, FEPI has only 15 names, which it tracks and against which it sells covered calls.

As we know, this year has been about the great rotation from growth to value and investors de-risking their portfolios amidst rising risks. Consequently, it is not only the aggressive yield space that has sold off (think of private credit through the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) and equity CLOs like Oxford Lane Capital Corporation (OXLC)), but also richly priced growth names.

Here are just three concrete examples: Palantir Technologies, Inc. (PLTR), Oracle Corporation (ORCL), and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), which are included in FEPI's portfolio and which plunged this year:

Given that for FEPI the average total portfolio exposure to a single security is around 6.5%, each such kind of event renders tangible damage to its performance.

Now, you might wonder about some other FEPI holdings, such as Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) and Intel Corporation (INTC), which have gone ballistic this year. Holdings like these should theoretically offset the losers.

It is only partially true, which leads me to the second point.

Second, since FEPI is a covered call ETF, it automatically introduces a cap on the underlying holdings as to how far they can appreciate without FEPI not foregoing the upside. For example, Intel's share price has surged by ~80%, where FEPI has certainly not been able to capture the majority of this upside.

We can take actually a look at what level of INTC's upside has been left uncaptured by FEPI. Currently, Intel trades at ~$81 per share, while FEPI has sold covered calls based on the strike of $70 per share (INTC US 05/15/26 C70).

As a result of this unfavorable asymmetry, where the downside is left almost fully open (slightly offset by the collected option premiums), but the upside is limited, FEPI has not been able to fully harvest the benefit of its certain securities, which have exploded higher this year. Instead, it has almost fully absorbed the negatives stemming from Palantir, Oracle, etc.

Now, if we look at the dividends, then, as mentioned above, they have decreased accordingly.

For some it might come as a surprise given the elevated VIX, which is inherently accommodative for option premiums.

This is an excellent moment for all covered call ETF investors to realize that the performance of the underlying holdings plays a way more important role for dividend stability and growth than volatility.

Simply put, if the underlying principal declines, the notional exposure against which the covered calls are written shrinks accordingly, which, in turn, reduces the total premium amount (in a more meaningful fashion than rising VIX provides benefits).

Another point that confirms this is the fact that the most recent dividend payment has finally increased, which coincides with the recovery in both the broader equity markets and, of course, FEPI's holdings base. Meanwhile, VIX has actually decreased a bit (take a look at the very first chart of this article).

With this valuable context and observations in mind, let me now summarize and share my investment stance on FEPI.

The Bottom Line

These recent months have proved that:

a) FEPI is a high-beta stock, which means that in case of a system-wide downside, investors will experience unfavorable returns.

b) Even if VIX spikes when the sell-off happens, it will most likely not enhance the option premiums to the extent that it would be necessary for offsetting the dividend declines stemming from contracted underlying holdings.

c) FEPI's covered call ETF factor coupled with concentrated high-growth name investments will inevitably translate to a serious opportunity cost (i.e., foregone upside), especially in moments like for Intel, when the company enjoys a massive relief rally.

However, I have to say that I still like FEPI and consider it a strong candidate for any income investor's portfolio.

Here is why.

Even though the distributions have been fluctuating, the yield has remained in the 20% to 25% range, which I think is impressive given the notable sell-off that we have experienced. On top of that, if we annualize the most recent dividend payment, we will arrive at ~25.2% yield, where the underlying payments will likely march higher as the sector AI-linked names recover. In other words, the income volatility is immaterial, and, importantly, it tends to recover together with FEPI's investments.

Furthermore, income investors have a chance to access select high-quality and high-potential AI names without losing cash flows. Namely, through FEPI, investors can get both worlds: enticing dividends and AI-linked exposures.

Last but not least, I would like to highlight the same (but very important) thing that I did in my previous piece:

If one believes that we are close to entering a deep and prolonged bear market, then the chances are very high that the dividend will decrease together with the ETF's share price. And the emphasis here is on the word "prolonged", which would disable a smooth and quick recovery process that, for example, happened right after the tariff-tantrum.

The recent shock, which was associated with the war in Iran and surging oil prices, was a good candidate for punishing the markets and leaving them down for a prolonged period of time. However, just as it has pretty much always been the case over the past couple of years, the market has once again recovered relatively quickly.

My view is that we will have a heightened volatility, where the markets will be punctuated by periodic sell-offs and relatively quick recoveries. If this is what you also see as a base case, then FEPI should definitely be considered as an addition to your income-oriented portfolios.

data.title: Annaly Capital Earnings: The 12% Yield Is Safe
data.description:
Last week, Annaly Capital (NLY) released its earnings for the first quarter of 2026. The release was a mixed one on the surface: adjusted earnings beat expectations, while revenue and reported earnings both missed. Trading in NLY stock after its earnings came out was consistent with the “mixed” nature of the results, with the price barely moving in the days after the release came out.

Regardless of Annaly’s lukewarm performance post earnings, the release was a very strong one in one critical way:

It showed that NLY stock’s dividend was quite well supported by cash flows.

In recent years, dividend sustainability has been a major concern for Annaly. The company cut its dividend twice in recent memory: first in 2020, under pandemic-related earnings pressure, and then again in 2023, due to a decline in earnings available for distribution. This history of cuts has made many income-oriented investors wary of investing in Annaly, despite its girthy dividend payout.

In Q1, Annaly did $0.76 in adjusted earnings per share while paying out $0.70 in common dividends. The company’s preferred dividends totalled $40.65 million, or $0.056 for each common share. Therefore, Annaly earned more than enough in the quarter to pay for both common and preferred dividends. This indicates that the company’s dividend is not presently at risk and that NLY stock’s high yield is fairly safe.

When I last covered Annaly Capital, I rated the stock a buy because its yield appeared both high and sustainable. Since then, NLY stock has delivered an underwhelming performance, while its dividend has been maintained, as my thesis predicted it would. Today, investors have the opportunity to buy NLY stock more cheaply than they could have a few months ago, despite its earnings growing and its dividend safety improving in the period. Accordingly, I am maintaining my NLY rating at buy.

Annaly Capital Basics

Annaly Capital is one of the world’s biggest mortgage REITs, a major investor in mortgage-backed securities [MBS] sold by entities like Fannie Mae. Its portfolio consists of mortgages packaged into securities, yielding about 6% on average, with the yield increased through the use of leverage. Annaly’s use of leverage might look risky; however, the company’s portfolio consists almost entirely of agency MBS, which are insured by the issuers. So, Annaly’s strategy is not as risky as it appears.

Through the use of leverage, Annaly’s portfolio managers are able to achieve a 12.31% interest rate. The way it works is Annaly borrows money at rates lower than MBS yields, then invests the funds into MBS, pocketing the spread. This is a fairly simple strategy, one that should, in theory, continue to work out.

Is it actually working out in reality? To answer that question, we can turn to the company’s most recent earnings release.

Earnings Recap

Annaly Capital’s first quarter earnings release, as mentioned previously, was mixed. The results beat expectations on a few metrics while missing on others. However, the release was positive for anybody hoping to simply collect the dividend, as it showed the company’s dividend sustainability improving. Here are some key metrics revealed in Annaly Capital’s Q1 release:

  • Interest income: $1.72 billion, up 31% year over year [y/y].

  • Interest expense: $1.27 billion, up 16% y/y.

  • Net interest income: $452.69 million, up 106% y/y.

  • Net servicing income: $52 million, up 8.3% y/y.

  • Net income: $290 million, up 123% y/y.

  • Reported earnings per share [EPS]: $0.33, up 120% y/y.

  • Earnings available for distribution: $0.76, up 5.5% y/y.

These results were broadly in line with expectations, with adjusted EPS slightly ahead of estimates and reported [GAAP] EPS slightly behind them. Viewed in absolute terms, the release was bullish. First, it showed high growth from a company that trades at just 7.7 times earnings and 1.15 times book. The value/growth combination appears agreeable. Second, the company’s adjusted earnings were ahead of its dividend payouts, indicating a stable dividend. Given what we saw last quarter, NLY shareholders can likely expect their dividends to continue being paid going forward. That’s a major plus for a stock with a 12.31% yield!

Speaking of Annaly's yield, let's take a look at where it comes from.

How Annaly Achieves Its 12.31% Yield

Annaly Capital pays a $0.70 quarterly dividend, for a total of $2.80 in dividends per year. At today’s stock price ($22.75), the yield is 12.31%.

When you look at an asset yielding 12.31%, there are two questions you should ask yourself:

  1. Is the company taking on too much risk to earn this yield?

  2. Is the yield likely to persist into the future?

To answer these questions about Annaly, we need to explore the company’s use of leverage as well as its financial picture.

Use of leverage

Currently, U.S. mortgages yield 6.23% on average. The yield has been pretty stable over the last 12 months, a period in which the Federal Reserve [Fed] has mostly held rates steady.

Annaly Capital's 12.31% yield is nearly double that of the underlying MBS portfolio. Clearly, there are significant amounts of leverage used to make this happen. We should ask whether this leverage is risky or not. Before getting into that, let's explore how leverage increases yield in general.

Let’s say you have $1,000,000 to invest in a 6% yielding collection of mortgages, and a $6,000,000 line of credit fixed at 5%. If you invested without leverage, you’d get a 6.23% yield. If, on the other hand, you fully tapped the $6,000,000 line of credit, you’d have a 12% yield.

Here’s how that would work. By investing an additional $6,000,000 worth of borrowed funds into your MBS portfolio, you would keep your “shareholder’s equity” the same at $1,000,000. However, the $6,000,000 leveraged portion of your portfolio would bring in an additional $60,000 in income. The $60,000 comes from $360,000 in annual interest income minus $300,000 in annual interest expense. The 6% income on your unleveraged portfolio is $60,000. So, in total, you’d bring in $120,000 against equity of $1 million, for a 12% yield.

Annaly Capital: Key Financial Metrics

According to Seeking Alpha Quant, Annaly Capital had $117.26 billion in net debt vs. $16.325 billion in equity last quarter. This is a debt-to-equity ratio of seven, pretty close to what I outlined in my hypothetical example.

A debt-to-equity ratio of seven is considered high in several industries, but is actually low by financial standards. Banks usually have “debts” (i.e., deposits) worth tens of times their shareholders' equity. This is considered normal for the banking sector because in that sector, liabilities drive revenue directly--quite different from how things work in a sector like manufacturing. A manufacturer who borrows $1 billion to buy land, may never find a revenue-generating purpose for that land. Banks, dealing primarily in liquid assets, can turn debt into revenue quickly.

Annaly Capital is not a bank, but it’s quite similar to one in its structure. It borrows money and immediately invests the funds into securities; specifically, MBS. As long as there is a reasonable spread between mortgage rates and Annaly’s borrowing rate, the use of leverage is not especially risk.

What is Annaly’s borrowing rate?

According to Seeking Alpha’s NLY financial statements, the company paid $4.75 billion in interest on $119.60 billion in net debt in the TTM period. It earned $6.37 billion in interest in the same period. So, NLY had a 3.97% effective interest rate and a 1.35% interest rate spread in the TTM period. This is a recipe for continued profitability in the year ahead.

Valuation

Having looked at Annaly’s overall strategy and recent earnings, we can now move on to valuing its stock. Usually I use multiples as part of my valuation discussion, however, since I already mentioned NLY’s P/E ratio earlier in the article, I will move straight into a discounted cash flow [DCF] valuation.

Annaly Capital had $0.76 in earnings available for distribution last quarter. This annualizes to $3.04 per year. The company’s historical growth rates have been extremely erratic and unpredictable (see table below), so I will not assume any future growth in this case. Annaly’s use of leverage and history of dividend cuts make it somewhat risky, so I’ll use a high risk premium here, resulting in a 10% discount rate. $3.04 divided by 0.1 is $30.40. This is much higher than NLY’s current stock price, $22.75, giving NLY upside of 33.6%. So, Annaly Capital stock appears to be worth the price of admission today.

Risks

As we’ve seen, Annaly Capital is performing well and exceeding earnings expectations. It certainly looks like a good opportunity. Nevertheless, there are risks to keep in mind, including:

  • Financing costs. Annaly’s whole business depends on it being able to borrow for less than a typical mortgage interest rate. Were the company’s financing costs ever to rise higher than a mortgage rate, it would struggle to find good investment opportunities. Companies’ financing costs sometimes do go higher than typical mortgage rates. It often happens when companies default on their bonds. So, credit risk is a factor Annaly investors will need to monitor. A credit event would have seriously negative consequences here.

  • Real estate market risks. While the U.S. homeowner is quite robust right now, with record high levels of equity, deterioration in his/her finances is a risk. Were U.S. homeowners to default in large numbers, Annaly’s portfolio income would decline significantly. The agency MBS portfolio is insured, which provides principal protection. That is a big plus. But it’s not enough to ensure a positive return in all possible scenarios.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on Annaly Capital’s first-quarter earnings release is that it showed a financially robust company that is more than capable of paying its dividend. With the stock yielding 12.31%, that alone is enough to justify an investment in NLY.

data.title: Settling Into Uncertainty
data.description:
Real Estate Weekly Outlook

U.S. equity markets advanced for a fourth straight week—notching a fresh series of record highs—as investors cheered a solid start to earnings season, resilient retail sales data, and increased clarity on the central bank front, largely shrugging off a renewed surge in oil prices and mounting signs of economic headwinds in more oil-exposed international markets. Markets navigated a complex crosscurrent of resilient corporate earnings alongside a fluid geopolitical backdrop marked by an extended ceasefire punctuated by on-again, off-again peace talks and continued uncertainty surrounding shipping activity through the Strait of Hormuz. Meanwhile, investors digested incremental clarity on the future leadership of the Federal Reserve, as the DOJ formally dropped its probe into Jerome Powell, clearing the path for confirmation hearings for Kevin Warsh as a potential successor and a final farewell press conference from Chair Powell in the week ahead.

Advancing for a fourth straight week, the S&P 500 edged higher by 0.6%, extending its streak of record highs as investors continued to lean into a constructive earnings backdrop. The tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 led the way with a 2.3% weekly advance, powered by strength in semiconductor names following upbeat results from resurgent chip maker Intel. Growth stocks extended their winning streak over value to a fourth straight week, further eroding value’s year-to-date performance advantage. The Mid-Cap 400 slipped 0.2% on the week, while the Dow also finished modestly lower. Pressured by the jump in benchmark rates, real estate equities lagged despite a solid start to earnings season. The Equity REIT Index slipped 1.4%, with 13 of 18 property sectors in negative territory, while Mortgage REITs eked out a gain of 0.1%. The Housing Index finished flat, as a rebound from single-family homebuilders was offset by softness from residential REITs ahead of a jam-packed slate of earnings results in the week ahead.

In bond markets, the rebound in oil prices, coupled with resilient economic data, renewed upward pressure on Treasuries this week, nudging yields modestly higher ahead of the upcoming Fed decision. WTI Crude Oil jumped more than 10% on the week to around $95 per barrel, reversing a portion of the prior week’s decline. Despite increased clarity around the central bank’s leadership outlook—an outcome that, in a quieter week, may have driven a more pronounced dovish response—markets were largely unmoved, with both rates and risk assets showing a notably muted reaction. The 10-Year Treasury Yield rose 6 basis points on the week to 4.31% - at the upper end of its 2026 range between 3.96% and 4.44%. The Fed-sensitive 2-Year Treasury Yield rose 7 basis points to 3.78% - also on the upper end of its 2026 range of 3.38% to 3.98%. Swaps markets now price a median Fed Funds Rate of 3.64% by the end of 2026, implying roughly 0.4 rate cuts by year-end, a slightly more hawkish view compared to the 0.6 cuts implied last week. The VIX volatility index was little changed on the week, holding near its lowest levels since early February. Snapping a three-week rally, Gold prices dipped 3% on the week, while the U.S. Dollar rebounded by 0.5%.

Real Estate Economic Data

Below, we recap the most important economic data points over this past week affecting the residential and commercial real estate marketplace.

Equity REIT & Homebuilder Week In Review

Best & Worst Performance This Week Across the REIT Sector

Real estate earnings season kicked into gear this week, with results from nearly two dozen REITs, highlighted by continued strength from net lease and retail REITs, a record-setting quarter of leasing activity from data center REITs, and mixed results from mortgage REITs. As discussed in our Earnings Preview, REITs entered earnings season with some upside momentum despite renewed interest rate volatility, underscored by renewed IPO and M&A activity across the sector. We highlighted several encouraging green shoots that have emerged in recent months, including surprisingly resilient travel demand, continued strength in retail leasing activity, and a return of some "animal spirits." Early signs of stabilization are also emerging in previously supply-pressured property sectors, including office, industrial, and life sciences, as shrinking development pipelines and moderating construction activity begin to rebalance supply and demand. Weaker trends persist across housing-related sectors, including soft residential rent growth in supply-heavy Sunbelt markets, subdued housing turnover weighing on self-storage demand, and elevated interest-rate volatility pressuring mortgage REIT performance. Below, we discussed the highlights and lowlights of the past week.

Healthcare: On cue with the M&A theme, Sila Realty Trust (SILA) surged nearly 20% this week after announcing that it has agreed to be acquired by private equity firm Blue Owl Capital in an all-cash $2.4B deal at $30.38 per share - a 19% premium to its prior closing price. SILA owns 137 healthcare properties across 65 U.S. markets, focused primarily on medical office facilities. SILA was among the newest public REITs, having completed a June 2024 direct listing on the NYSE after operating for years as a non-traded REIT. The deal adds to a growing wave of M&A activity across the REIT sector, reflecting both renewed investor confidence in real estate fundamentals and the persistence of valuation gaps in public markets. SILA becomes the sixth public REIT to agree to an acquisition in just the past quarter, joining deals involving Whitestone, Two Harbors, National Storage, Veris Residential, Peakstone, and Apollo Commercial. Notably, five of these six transactions involve private equity buyers, underscoring the continued appetite from institutional investors for stabilized real estate platforms trading below private-market values. More broadly, the deal extends a consolidation trend that has accelerated since mid-2025. Over that period, 15 public REITs have agreed to be acquired, including 12 by private investment firms and three by larger public REITs pursuing scale or portfolio repositioning. While these deals shrink the number of publicly traded REITs, they also serve as an important validation of underlying real estate values. For Blue Owl, the acquisition marks another expansion of its rapidly growing real assets platform with investments across industrial properties, data centers, and real estate credit strategies.

Sticking with the healthcare M&A theme, National Healthcare Properties (NHP) saw a lukewarm market response in its public debut. NHP completed an initial public offering this week on the Nasdaq exchange, raising $462M in common equity via a 38.5 million share offering priced at $12 per share - below the $13 to $16 range indicated last week. NHP closed the week at $12.85, translating to a market value of roughly $730M. Founded in 2012, NHP operated as a non-traded REIT sponsored by Healthcare Trust Advisors - part of a group of non-traded REITs and business development companies sponsored by American Realty Capital ("ARC"), a private investment platform led by Nicholas Schorsch. The ARC complex has delivered a mixed track record, with notable successes including the turnaround of VEREIT—ultimately acquired by Realty Income in 2021—and more recent progress at Global Net Lease, alongside a more uneven history across other sponsored vehicles. NHP owns 167 healthcare properties across 29 states, including 37 senior housing communities and 130 outpatient medical buildings. NHI plans to use the IPO proceeds primarily to reduce its debt. Its current Net Debt to Adjusted EBITDA Ratio is around 9x, well above the REIT sector average of 5-6x. Outpatient Medical Facilities (medical office buildings) account for roughly 63% of NHP's portfolio, with the remaining 37% consisting of Senior Housing Operating Properties (SHOP), primarily assisted living and memory care communities operated under RIDEA structures with third-party operators.

Office: Back on the earnings front, Philly-focused Brandywine (BDN) was the top post-earnings performer this week, rallying 3% after reporting decent results and reaffirming a constructive full-year outlook, consistent with a gradual stabilization across its urban office portfolio. Management maintained its 2026 FFO outlook, implying roughly 5.8% growth—marking what would be the company’s first year of positive FFO growth since 2019—as leasing activity and capital recycling efforts continue to gain traction. Leasing momentum was a notable bright spot, with BDN signing 268k square feet during the quarter—about 35% above its trailing twelve-month pace—with BDN citing a continued “flight-to-quality” demand in its core Philadelphia markets. Portfolio occupancy held steady at 88.3%, consistent with prior quarter levels. Cash leasing spreads dipped to -2.6%; however, following a strong +10% mark-to-market in Q4. On the capital side, BDN highlighted continued progress on asset sales and deleveraging, with over $300M of dispositions under agreement expected to close in the near term, supporting needed balance sheet improvement. In our Earnings Preview, we analyzed recent nationwide data from JLL showing continued upside momentum in office leasing and occupancy trends. Net absorption was positive for the third consecutive quarter in Q1, led by rebounds in San Francisco and New York. Meanwhile, the supply backdrop continues to tighten significantly. U.S. office inventory declined by 9M square feet in Q1, bringing cumulative reductions since the 2023 peak to more than 25 million square feet, as conversions and demolitions outpace new deliveries.

Casino: Also on the upside this week, Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI) - the second-largest casino REIT - advanced 1% after delivering a strong quarter, highlighted by accretive external investments and another upward revision to its full-year outlook. GLPI raised its full-year 2026 AFFO growth outlook to 5.7% (from 5.3% previously), reflecting continued momentum across its acquisition and development pipeline and supported by contractual rent escalators and contributions from recent transactions. GLPI completed $727M of acquisitions in Q1, including the Bally’s Lincoln property and land for the Live! Casino & Hotel Virginia development—both at roughly 8% cap rates. GLPI commented that the transaction market has become more active, with a robust pipeline and “very active dialogue” across potential deals. Development activity also continues to ramp up, with roughly $1.8B of committed capital expected to be deployed through 2027 and full-year 2026 development spending projected at $750M–$800M. On the call, GLPI struck a constructive tone on the broader gaming environment, noting improving trends in regional markets following a softer 2025, with early-2026 results from operators indicating stabilization in demand and margins. GLPI also acknowledged some turbulence in credit markets for gaming operators, which is helping sustain attractive deal spreads and reinforces its role as a preferred capital provider.

Data Center: Digital Realty (DLR) - the second-largest data center REIT - slipped 2% this week despite a strong 1Q26 print, highlighted by record leasing activity and upward revisions to its full-year outlook. Fueled by continued AI-driven demand and hyperscale cloud expansion, DLR raised its full-year 2026 FFO growth outlook to 8.9% (up from 7.6%) and boosted revenue and EBITDA targets, citing strong commencements and a rapidly expanding backlog. Leasing trends remained exceptionally robust, with total bookings of $423M in Q1 - its second-highest ever - including its largest lease in company history (a 200 MW AI-focused hyperscale deal). DLR's backlog surged 26% sequentially to $1.0B at DLR’s share, with roughly half of this pipeline slated to commence in 2026. Rent growth moderated slightly in Q1, with cash renewal spreads of 5.0%, but DLR increased its full-year renewal spread outlook to 7.5% (from 7.0%), signaling confidence in sustained pricing power—particularly for larger, AI-driven deployments. DLR continues to aggressively expand its development pipeline, now totaling roughly 6 GW of future capacity, which is 60% pre-leased. On the call, DLR emphasized that AI demand is shifting from pilot to production, driving materially larger workloads and capacity needs, while also acknowledging ongoing constraints around power availability, labor, and supply chain dynamics.

Industrial: Following a strong start to earnings season last week from sector stalwart Prologis (PLD), results from a trio of industrial REITs this week reflected a generally constructive operating backdrop nationally but revealed pockets of softness in select tariff-exposed markets. West Coast-focused Rexford Industrial (REXR) slipped 4% this week after reporting mixed results, as upward revisions to full-year FFO and NOI guidance buoyed by stock buybacks were offset by a sharply negative re-leasing spread—the first negative quarterly spread reported by a major industrial REIT in over a decade. Supported by $200M of stock buybacks during the quarter, REXR raised its full-year 2026 Core FFO outlook, now expecting a modest -0.2% decline (from -1.0% previously), alongside an improved same-store NOI forecast of -1.0% (from -1.5%). REXR executed a record 4.1M square feet of leasing—well above its TTM average—supported by strong renewal volumes and improved tenant engagement as management prioritizes occupancy. However, cash leasing spreads were -15.4% (or -1.8% excluding a large renewal), while portfolio occupancy declined modestly to 96.3%, reflecting continued pressure from falling market rents and negative net absorption across Southern California. REXR estimates that market rents declined roughly 7% year-over-year (and ~1% sequentially), with Greater Los Angeles down closer to 9%. Earnings call commentary pointed to a still-fragile but improving demand backdrop shaped in part by recent tariff-related volatility. REXR indicated that it has not yet seen a meaningful demand impact from current geopolitical tensions but acknowledged that market fundamentals are still under pressure, with negative net absorption across Southern California.

Also under pressure this week, First Industrial (FR) - which owns a regionally diverse portfolio - declined 4% after trimming its full-year FFO outlook to reflect costs tied to a contested proxy process. FR is facing a contested proxy initiated by activist investor Land & Buildings, which cites a "persistent valuation gap" and argues that the company’s stock trades at a significant discount to its net asset value and peers. Property-level metrics were steady, however, with FR maintaining its same-store NOI growth at 5.5%, while leasing fundamentals remained solid, with sector-leading cash spreads of 32.0%. On the call, FR reported steady tenant demand trends, particularly among third-party logistics ("3PL") tenants, with improving touring activity and continued momentum in smaller space leasing, though decision-making for larger users remains somewhat slower. Elsewhere, Sunbelt-focused EastGroup (EGP) was the outperformer of the group after reporting a strong quarter highlighted by a healthy guidance boost. EGP raised its full-year FFO growth outlook to 6.5% and boosted same-store NOI guidance to 6.2%. Development activity remains a key driver, with improved lease-up progress, though management did guide to some modest occupancy drift through the balance of the year as new deliveries come online. Overall, results point to continued strength in Sunbelt industrial demand, particularly across smaller-bay logistics space where supply remains relatively constrained.

Net Lease: Net Lease REITs were among the laggards this week despite a wave of guidance increases, as upside was capped by an active quarter of capital raising, with companies taking advantage of improved equity valuations. Essential Properties (EPRT) declined 6% despite raising its 2026 FFO growth outlook to 7.1% and reporting record acquisition volume of $389M at a 7.7% cap rate. Investors focused on some modest softening in some tenant-related metrics. Unit-level rent coverage declined 10 basis points sequentially to a still-healthy 3.5x, while sub-1.0x exposure ticked up to 3.4% of ABR, driven by some pockets of softness within casual dining and entertainment. Agree Realty (ADC) declined nearly 4% after maintaining its full-year outlook, as recent forward equity offerings offset strong underlying results highlighted by a robust quarter of acquisition activity. ADC reported $424M in acquisitions during the quarter—its most active acquisition period since 2022—at a 7.1% cap rate, consistent with last quarter. Operationally, same-store rent growth accelerated to 1.6%, recapture rates exceeded 104%, and occupancy climbed to 99.7%, underscoring continued tenant demand and portfolio resilience. On the earnings call, ADC emphasized that tenant demand has not softened despite macro volatility and, in some cases, is accelerating. ADC noted “absolutely no hesitancy” from retail partners—even amid geopolitical uncertainty— and highlighted that store expansion remains a core strategic priority for leading retailers. ADC also struck a constructive tone on real estate markets more broadly, noting stable cap rates and no meaningful change in transaction dynamics over the past 18–24 months, despite rate volatility.

Sticking in the net lease space, Getty Realty (GTY) - which focuses on gas stations and other auto-related retail formats - slipped 3% despite boosting its full-year outlook, now expecting 2026 FFO growth of 2.9% (up from 2.0%). While acquisition activity was modest at $30M—below its recent run rate—management emphasized a robust pipeline, including roughly $125M under contract and additional opportunities beyond that level. Credit trends remain benign, with no realized losses in the quarter and no assets on a watch list. Netstreit (NTST) slipped 1% despite also boosting its 2026 FFO growth outlook to 5.4% at the midpoint (up from 4.8%) while increasing net investment guidance by 50% to $600M. Portfolio quality trends remained favorable, with rent coverage improving to 3.9x and occupancy holding near-perfect at 99.9%. However, the upside to guidance was tempered by dilution from treasury stock issuance, highlighting the tradeoff between growth and per-share earnings. Finally, small-cap Alpine Income (PINE) was the top performer of the group this week after delivering another strong quarter and boosting its full-year FFO growth outlook to a sector-leading 12.7%. PINE's portfolio consists of 125 properties, which are 99.5% occupied, with 50% of its rent coming from investment-grade rated tenants.

Mortgage: The initial slate of mortgage REIT results this week highlighted a volatile quarter across the sector, as swings in rates, spreads, and macro conditions weighed on near-term book values but simultaneously created a more attractive setup for forward returns for those REITs in a position to capitalize. On the upside, agency-focused mREITs AGNC Investment (AGNC) and Dynex Capital (DX) each gained 1% after reporting decent results that showed improved earnings power despite a rate-driven drop in Book Values. AGNC reported comparable EPS of $0.42 per share - up from $0.35 last quarter - as higher asset yields reflected improved carry across the agency MBS portfolio. As expected, Book Values declined amid widening mortgage spreads late in the quarter, tied to escalating geopolitical risk, but recovered most of the losses in the first three weeks of April. Orchid Island (ORC) also eked out a gain this week after reporting an increasingly attractive forward outlook for agency MBS investing, with management citing modeled return expectations of 15%–17%. Meanwhile, Annaly Capital (NLY) and ARMOUR Residential (ARR) each finished roughly flat after reporting in-line results. On the commercial side, Ladder Capital (LADR) reported a solid quarter highlighted by its highest quarterly loan origination volume in four years. LADR closed $621M in originations in 1Q, citing a more favorable lending backdrop, with wider spreads and less competition enabling more attractive risk-adjusted returns. On the downside this week, KKR Real Estate (KREF) dipped 10% after reporting another turbulent quarter marked by further credit deterioration and slashed its dividend by 60%. Portfolio trends remained challenged, with four additional risk rating downgrades during the quarter—including a Boston life science asset moved to the highest-risk category—and multiple office and multifamily exposures moving onto the watch list.

Manufactured Housing: Equity LifeStyle (ELS) - the second-largest manufactured housing REIT - slipped 3% this week after reporting in-line results, as continued softness in its RV segment offset strength in its core manufactured housing segment. ELS maintained its full-year FFO growth outlook at 3.6% but raised its same-store NOI growth outlook by 10 basis points to 5.7%, reflecting improved expense trends, including an 18% decline in insurance premiums tied to the modest hurricane season. The offset came from a slightly weaker revenue outlook, driven by softer expectations in seasonal and transient RV demand and delays in marina slip restorations. ELS noted that domestic RV demand has held up reasonably well, with recent softness tied to headwinds from international and seasonal demand disruptions, particularly from Canadian travelers and shifting reservation patterns following last year’s geopolitical and tariff-related frictions. Trends in the Annual RV segment—representing the majority of RV revenue—remain solid, with stable rent growth and improving attrition trends after elevated turnover last year. On the call, ELS highlighted the durability of its MH platform, which benefits from 97% homeowner residency and long tenure. ELS also emphasized its compelling affordability advantage, with MH homes priced at a fraction of single-family alternatives across key Sunbelt markets.

Cell Tower: Wrapping up the earnings analysis this week, Crown Castle (CCI) - the second-largest cell tower REIT - slipped about 3% after delivering a “status quo” report. Full-year guidance was unchanged, with FFO expected to increase by 3.3% this year, as DISH-related churn and muted leasing activity continue to weigh on near-term growth. Organic growth trends were slightly better than expected, but leasing volumes remain soft and back-half weighted. Strategically, CCI emphasized 2026 as a “transformative” year, with the fiber and small-cell divestiture on track for a mid-year close and a sharpened focus on becoming a pure-play U.S. tower operator. Post-close capital allocation remains centered on balance sheet strengthening and shareholder returns, including a planned $1B buyback alongside significant debt reduction. CCI noted that the fiber divestiture remains on track to close by mid-2026 and highlighted longer-term growth opportunities—including spectrum-driven leasing demand, new tower builds, and edge data centers—though near-term contributions are expected to be limited.

In Other REIT News:

  • Industrial Logistics Properties (ILPT) completed a significant refinancing, pricing $1.62B of five-year interest-only fixed-rate mortgage financing at 5.71% for its Mountain Industrial REIT JV, with proceeds used to refinance $1.4B of floating-rate debt due March 2027.
  • National Health Investors (NHI) entered into an agreement to sell a portfolio of 32 skilled nursing and three independent living facilities to National HealthCare Corporation for $560M and expects to use proceeds to repay debt and fund future investments.
  • Highwoods Properties (HIW) authorized a new $250M share repurchase program and expects to fund repurchases on a leverage-neutral basis using proceeds from the sale of non-core assets.
  • VICI Properties (VICI) entered into a new triple-net lease agreement with a Clairvest affiliate for Northfield Park and amended its master lease with MGM Resorts following the sale of MGM Northfield Park operations.
  • Moody’s upgraded the credit ratings of American Tower (AMT) to “Baa2” from “Baa3” and revised its outlook to Stable from Positive.
  • Fermi (FRMI) announced major leadership changes, including co-founder Toby Neugebauer stepping down as CEO while remaining on the Board.

2026 YTD Performance & 2025 Recap

Approaching the end of April, equity REITs have managed to hold onto their outperformance over the large-cap benchmarks despite the volatility in benchmark interest rates. The Equity REIT Index has gained 8.8% this year, while the Mortgage REIT Index has gained 4.3%, and the Housing 100 Index is flat on the year. This compares to the 5.0% gain for the S&P 500, the 10.4% gain for the Mid-Cap 400, and the 13.5% gain for the Small-Cap 600. Within the REIT sector, 17 of 20 property sectors are higher on the year, led by Data Center, Self-Storage, and Farmland REITs. At 4.31%, the 10-Year Treasury Yield is now higher by 15 basis points on the year, while the 2-Year Treasury Yield is higher by 31 basis points at 3.78%. Rates at the longest end of the yield curve have risen more modestly, with the 30-Year Treasury Yield higher by 7 basis points to 4.91%. The aggregate Bloomberg US Bond Index is higher by 0.6% this year after notching gains of 7% in 2025. In commodities markets, WTI Crude Oil has surged by 90% this year to around $95/barrel after dipping 12% in 2025. The US Dollar is roughly flat, while Gold remains higher by 9% despite some early-year weakness, and Bitcoin has dipped about 10% after declining 6% in 2025.

Economic Calendar In The Week Ahead

Investors should brace for a jam-packed week of market-moving newsflow in the week ahead, highlighted by the U.S. Federal Reserve's Rate Decision on Wednesday. As noted above, markets expect the Fed to hold rates steady for a third straight meeting following a stretch of three straight rate cuts from September through December. Investors will be closely focused on commentary from Jerome Powell in what is likely to be his final scheduled press conference before his term expires in May, with lingering uncertainty over whether he will remain on the FOMC thereafter—potentially breaking with recent precedent. We'll see a pair of key housing market indicators before the Fed meeting. On Tuesday, we'll see the Case-Shiller Home Price Index for February, which is expected to show continued flatlining in home price appreciation. Critically necessary to close the affordability gap, national home price appreciation has averaged just 3.5% annually since the start of 2023, compared to a 7.0% average annual increase in disposable personal incomes. On Wednesday, we'll see Housing Starts and Building Permits data for March, which are expected to show moderate levels of activity amid lingering pressure from elevated mortgage rates. After the Fed decision, we'll see a key inflation report on Thursday via the PCE Price Index for March. Tracking the CPI and PPI reports released earlier this month, surging oil prices are expected to drive headline PCE inflation to jump into the mid-3% range. We'll also get the first look at first-quarter Gross Domestic Product, which is expected to show the U.S. economy expanded at a 2.2% annualized rate in Q1, roughly in line with the 2.0% full-year increase in GDP in 2025. We'll also see a full slate of regional and national PMI reports throughout the week, including S&P's Manufacturing PMI and Services PMI report on Friday, and we will also be watching the weekly employment metrics via ADP and Jobless Claims.

For an in-depth analysis of all real estate sectors, check out all of our quarterly reports: Apartments, Homebuilders, Manufactured Housing, Student Housing, Single-Family Rentals, Cell Towers, Casinos, Industrial, Data Centers, Malls, Healthcare, Net Lease, Shopping Centers, Hotels, Billboards, Office, Farmland, Storage, Timber, Mortgage, and Cannabis.

Disclosure: Hoya Capital Real Estate advises two Exchange-Traded Funds listed on the NYSE. In addition to any long positions listed below, Hoya Capital is long all components in the Hoya Capital Housing 100 Index and in the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield Index. Index definitions and a complete list of holdings are available on our website.
